g.data-narration: Welcome to CUDA Trapezoidal Rule I. We'll learn the basic atomicAdd approach for GPU numerical integration.
The trapezoidal rule approximates integrals by summing trapezoid areas between evenly spaced points.
g.text: where h = (b-a)/n, x_i = a + i×h for i = 0,1,...,n
● Key: All f(x_i) are INDEPENDENT — perfect for parallel execution!

g.data-narration: GPUs excel at this because every function evaluation is independent, providing uniform workload across thousands of cores.
g.text: Why It Parallelizes Well on GPU
Uniform workload — every thread does the same work
Large n (millions) — GPU has thousands of cores

g.data-narration: Each thread computes one f(x_i). The global thread index maps to the subinterval point using this formula.
g.text: Thread Mapping: One Thread Per Point

text.tspan: my_i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x // Global index

g.text: Each thread i computes f(x_i) and adds to global sum

g.data-narration: Here's the single-block CUDA kernel. Each thread calculates its x value, evaluates f(x), handles boundary weights, and atomically adds to the result.
g.text: __global__ void dev_trap(float a, float b, int n, float h, float* result_p) {

g.data-narration: The multi-block version extends to handle millions of points by computing global thread index from block and thread indices.
g.text: __global__ void dev_trap(float a, float b, int n, float h, float* result_p) {
int my_i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x; // KEY CHANGE!

g.data-narration: atomicAdd performs a thread-safe read-modify-write. The hardware serializes these operations to prevent race conditions.
g.text: No other thread can interfere between read and write — hardware uses compare-and-swap!

g.data-narration: The atomicAdd bottleneck serializes all writes, making accumulation O(n) instead of O(n/p). This negates GPU parallelism benefits.
g.text: Problem: When 1 million threads call atomicAdd to the SAME address:
● The speedup from parallel compute is lost in serial accumulation

g.data-narration: The host code allocates device memory, zeros it, launches the kernel, copies back the result, and multiplies by h.
g.text: ● Don't forget cudaMemset to 0 — otherwise you accumulate garbage!

g.data-narration: Due to thread scheduling variations, floating-point addition order differs between runs, producing slightly different results.
g.text: Thread execution order varies between runs → atomicAdd order varies
(a + b) + c ≠ a + (b + c) in floating-point
● Mitigation: Use double precision, or accept tiny differences (far smaller than O(h²) error)

g.data-narration: The atomicAdd approach is analogous to OpenMP critical sections — correct but slow, unlike optimized reduction.
g.text: Comparison: CUDA vs OpenMP vs MPI
● OpenMP reduction clause → optimized tree reduction (no serialization)
● CUDA Trapezoidal Rule II → shared-memory parallel reduction (fixes this!)

g.data-narration: Despite its limitations, atomicAdd works when n is small, f(x) is expensive, or code simplicity matters more than peak performance.
g.text: ● It's a pedagogical baseline before learning optimized shared-memory reduction

g.data-narration: Remember these pitfalls: forgetting cudaMemset, missing h multiplication, wrong grid size, and forgetting boundary weights.
This first version teaches correctness before performance. The atomicAdd approach is correct but slow, motivating the optimized reduction version.
g.text: ● Each thread computes f(x_i) independently — perfect GPU parallelism
● atomicAdd ensures correctness but serializes accumulation (performance bottleneck)
● Host workflow: cudaMalloc → cudaMemset(0) → kernel → cudaMemcpy → ×h
